layout.heading: Multi-pane Layout (Snap Layouts)
layout.p1: Drag a tab by its label and drop it on the left/right/top/bottom edge of any pane's content area to split the workspace and view two tabs side by side — drop on the edge of an existing pane again to split it further, nesting as many rows/columns as you need. A thin divider appears between panes; drag it to resize.
layout.p2: To merge panes back, drag a tab onto another pane's tab strip (or drop it in the middle of that pane's content area instead of an edge) — the tab moves there, and if that was the last tab in its old pane, the now-empty pane and its split collapse away automatically. Clicking anywhere in a pane focuses it, which is what <kbd>Ctrl+1</kbd>-<kbd>9</kbd>, <kbd>Ctrl+W</kbd>, <kbd>Ctrl+L</kbd>, and <kbd>Space</kbd> act on.
layout.p3: The Plotter is shared across the whole window rather than living inside one pane — toggle it from the top bar and pick its source tab from its own dropdown regardless of which pane that tab is currently showing in.

monitor.heading: Serial Monitor
monitor.intro: Click + in the tab strip (or Ctrl+N) to connect. Each connection is its own tab with independent settings. In the monitor toolbar:
monitor.li1: mixed / hex / ascii — switch how each line's bytes are displayed.
monitor.li2: delta / abs / off — timestamp relative to the first line, wall-clock time, or hidden.
monitor.li3: Pause (or Space) freezes the view without losing incoming data — Resume shows how many lines arrived while paused.
monitor.li4: The disk icon starts logging raw + timestamped output to a file, rotated at 50 MB (serial connections only).
monitor.li5: Lines matching common log formats (ESP-IDF's E (328) wifi: ..., [ERROR], or a plain WARN: prefix) are auto-colored by level — no setup needed, this runs alongside filters/search.
monitor.li6: The bar at the bottom of the monitor shows live bytes/s, lines/s, error count, and uptime for the tab.
monitor.li7: Everything you send (Send panel, quick commands, macros, scripts) is echoed into the log as its own line — marked with a » and shown in the accent color, vs « for lines received from the device — so a request and its reply both show up in the same buffer, in order.
monitor.autoReconnect: Enable Auto-reconnect when connecting over serial to have the tab reopen automatically if the device is unplugged and replugged (matched by USB vendor/product id + serial number, not the OS port name, which can change). If the connection drops, use Disconnect/Reconnect in the tab — your COM port, baud rate, and other settings stay filled in.

network.heading: TCP / UDP / WebSocket / MQTT Connections
network.intro: The New connection panel's segmented control isn't limited to Serial — pick TCP Client, TCP Server, UDP, WS Client, WS Server, or MQTT instead, and the tab that opens behaves exactly like a serial one: filters, triggers, macros, the script engine, and the plotter all work unmodified since they only ever see a stream of bytes, not the transport underneath.
network.tcpClient: TCP Client — connect out to a device's IP/port, e.g. a Wi-Fi module exposing a raw TCP console on 192.168.1.50:23.
network.tcpServer: TCP Server — listen on a local port and wait for the device to connect in instead (useful when the device only supports outbound connections). Handles one client at a time.
network.udp: UDP — bind a local port to receive on, and optionally set a remote host/port to send to. Broadcast addresses (e.g. 192.168.1.255) work too.
network.ws: WS Client — connect to a WebSocket endpoint by full URL, e.g. ws://192.168.1.1:81/ (an ESP32 running a WebSocket server is a common target). WS Server listens on a local port instead and waits for a client to connect in, handling one at a time like TCP Server. Incoming text messages appear as one monitor line each; binary messages pass through unmodified. Everything you send — text or hex — goes out as a binary WebSocket frame, so raw/binary protocols round-trip correctly; encrypted wss:// endpoints aren't supported yet.
network.mqtt: MQTT — connect to a broker (host/port, optional username/password), subscribe to a topic filter (e.g. # for everything, or devices/+/telemetry for one level of wildcard), and set a publish topic for the Send panel. Each incoming message appears as a topic: payload line; the client reconnects to the broker on its own if the connection drops, so brief network blips don't need any action from you.
network.mqttExample: Example — watch and command an MQTT device: broker broker.hivemq.com:1883, subscribe topic devices/kitchen-sensor/#, publish topic devices/kitchen-sensor/cmd. Incoming readings show up as devices/kitchen-sensor/temp: 24.5; typing reboot in the send box publishes it straight to the cmd topic.
network.findingDevices: Finding devices: for TCP Client, WS Client, and MQTT targets, the Scan LAN button below the connection fields browses the local network via mDNS/DNS-SD for a chosen service type (HTTP, MQTT, Arduino OTA, ESPHome, WebSocket) and lists what it finds — click a result to fill in the host and port automatically. Devices must advertise themselves via mDNS (most IoT firmware frameworks do) and be on the same subnet.

ssh.heading: SSH Terminal
ssh.p1: Pick SSH in the New connection panel and enter host, port, username, and password. Unlike the other connection kinds, an SSH tab opens a real interactive terminal (a PTY, rendered with an actual terminal emulator) instead of a line-oriented monitor — there's no raw-log toggle, no Send panel, and no filters/triggers/script engine, since those all assume a stream of discrete lines rather than a live shell. The terminal resizes itself (and tells the remote shell) whenever its pane changes size, including when you split or merge panes.
ssh.p2: While an SSH tab is focused, <kbd>Ctrl+W</kbd> and <kbd>Ctrl+L</kbd> go to the shell (delete word / clear screen) instead of being caught as the app's close-tab / clear-log shortcuts. Paste with <kbd>Ctrl+V</kbd> or right-click — both read from the system clipboard directly rather than relying on the browser's native paste, which isn't reliable inside the app's webview. Selecting text copies it automatically (no keyboard shortcut needed, since <kbd>Ctrl+C</kbd> sends SIGINT to the remote shell instead) — double-click selects a word, triple-click a whole line.
ssh.p3: A terminal has no concept of "click to move the cursor mid-command" the way a text editor does — the remote shell (not this app) owns the command line you're typing, so editing it works the same as any other terminal: <kbd>Home</kbd>/<kbd>End</kbd>, <kbd>Ctrl+←</kbd>/<kbd>Ctrl+→</kbd> to jump by word, and <kbd>Ctrl+A</kbd>/<kbd>Ctrl+E</kbd> to jump to the start/end of the line.
ssh.p4: Two current limitations worth knowing: authentication is password-only (no key-based auth yet), and the host key isn't verified against a known-hosts list — any server's key is accepted, so this isn't yet safe against an on-path attacker impersonating the host.

modbus.heading: RS485 & Modbus RTU
modbus.rs485: For RS485 transceivers whose DE/RE direction pin has no auto-direction circuitry (common on bare MAX485-style breakout boards, unlike most all-in-one USB-RS485 dongles), check RS485 half-duplex (auto RTS toggle) when connecting over Serial. RTS is then asserted right before every write and held until the bytes have actually finished shifting out at the configured baud rate, then released — timed from the baud rate and frame size, not a fixed guess, so the last byte doesn't get clipped. The manual RTS button in the signal bar is disabled while this is on, since toggling it by hand would fight the automatic control.
modbus.toolsIntro: Two sidebar tools speak Modbus on top of a connection:
modbus.master: Modbus Master (gauge icon) — build a one-shot request (slave address, function code, start address, quantity or value, timeout) against a real Modbus RTU device and see the parsed response, or set up poll rules to read a register repeatedly on an interval — each rule's value also feeds the Plotter automatically, using the rule's label as the channel name. Only one request is ever in flight on the bus at a time, whether it came from a manual click or a poll rule, since RS485 is half-duplex. The Master also works on a TCP Client tab: connect to a Modbus TCP device or gateway (port 502 by convention) and the same requests go out MBAP-framed as Modbus TCP — the "Slave address" field becomes the Unit ID. The Slave emulator remains serial-only.
modbus.slave: Modbus Slave (chip icon) — emulate a Modbus RTU device to test a real master against. Set a slave address, fill in whichever coils/discrete inputs/holding registers/input registers you want it to answer for, click Start listening, and it responds to matching requests automatically — including a spec-correct exception response if the master asks for a register you haven't added. It keeps listening in the background even if you switch to a different sidebar panel or tab. A tab can't run both Master polling and Slave listening at once — enabling one disables the other, since one RS485 bus can't sensibly be both roles at the same time.
modbus.example: Example — read holding register 0 from slave address 1: in Modbus Master, set slave address 1, function 03 Read Holding Registers, start address 0, quantity 1, then Send. On the wire this is the request 01 03 00 00 00 01 84 0A (the trailing two bytes are the CRC16). You can see this exact codec in action without any hardware at all: open Modbus Slave on a second tab connected to the other end of a loopback/null-modem pair, add holding register 0 with some value, enable Start listening, and send the request from the first tab's Master panel to watch the round trip end to end.

presets.heading: Saved Profiles, Scripts & Presets
presets.p1: Four places share the same small save/load control — a dropdown plus a disk icon (save) and trash icon (delete): the Profile picker in the New connection panel, Script in the script panel, and Preset in both Filters and Triggers. Picking an item from the dropdown loads it immediately; the disk icon prompts for a name and saves whatever is currently filled in (overwriting if you reuse a name); the trash icon deletes whichever item is selected. Everything is stored locally in the app, so it's there next time you open EDT — nothing to export/import manually.
presets.p2: Example: set up a TCP Client profile for your dev board once, save it as dev-board, and pick it from the Profile dropdown on any future connection instead of retyping the host/port. Same idea for a Lua script you use across multiple tabs, or a filter preset like ERROR|WARN + exclude heartbeat that you'd otherwise rebuild by hand each time.

projectProfiles.heading: Project Profiles
projectProfiles.p1: The folder icon in the top bar (Open project) and the disk icon next to it (Save project) save/restore the whole workspace to a .edtproj file on disk — every open tab's connection, its filters/triggers/script, the Snap Layout arrangement, and the Plotter's config (source tab, extractors, math channels, thresholds, chart type — not the plotted data itself, which repopulates once the source tab reconnects). Unlike the Profile/Script/Preset pickers above, this is a real file you can move between machines or hand to a teammate working on the same device, not something tucked away in local app storage.
projectProfiles.p2: Opening a project reconnects every saved connection under a new tab and closes whatever was open before — you'll be asked to confirm if you have tabs open already. SSH connections pause for a password prompt each time (passwords are never written to the file); a connection that fails or whose password prompt you cancel is simply left out of the restored layout rather than blocking the rest.

search.heading: Search & Bookmarks
search.p1: Press <kbd>Ctrl+F</kbd> to open an in-buffer regex search bar. <kbd>Enter</kbd> / <kbd>Shift+Enter</kbd> jump to the next/previous match; <kbd>Escape</kbd> closes it. Search runs over whatever filters currently have applied, so you can narrow down with a filter first and search within that subset.
search.p2: Click the bookmark icon on any line (or use a Bookmark line trigger action, see Triggers below) to mark it. The bookmark arrows in the toolbar jump between marked lines in order — handy for flagging a handful of interesting moments in a long capture and revisiting them without re-reading everything in between.

sendMacro.heading: Send Panel & Macro Recorder
sendMacro.p1: Type in the send box and press Enter, or switch to hex mode to send raw bytes like 01 02 FF. Pick the line ending (None/CR/LF/CRLF) once per tab. Arrow up/down recalls previous sends.
sendMacro.p2: The second dropdown next to Line Ending is Checksum — pick CRC16 (Modbus), CRC16 (CCITT), CRC8, XOR, or Sum, and it's appended to every message you send from this tab, in both text and hex mode (a Modbus RTU frame typed as hex, for instance, gets the correct CRC16 tacked on automatically). Turning a checksum on also suppresses the line ending for that send — a trailing CR/LF after a binary checksum would corrupt the frame — so there's nothing else to configure for binary protocols.
sendMacro.p3: The macro recorder lives in the sidebar next to Filters/Triggers/Script — click the repeat icon to open it.
sendMacro.exampleLabel: Example — record and replay a test sequence:
sendMacro.step2: Send AT from the send panel, wait a moment, then send AT+GMR.
sendMacro.step4: Click Play any time to replay both commands with the same real delay between them, or edit a step's delay / remove it right there in the panel.

quickCommands.heading: Quick Commands
quickCommands.p1: The slim row above the send box is for one-click, frequently-reused commands (think AT commands, a reboot string, a status query) — unlike the macro recorder, there's no sequence or delay involved, each chip just fires that one command immediately when clicked.
quickCommands.p2: Commands are grouped into named profiles (the dropdown on the left) so you can keep separate sets for different devices — saved permanently, and shared by every tab, not just the one you created them in. Pick a profile to show its commands; the + button next to the dropdown creates a new one.
quickCommands.li1: + Add at the end of the row opens a small form below for the command's label (optional — falls back to showing the command text itself), the text or hex payload to send, and a hex toggle.
quickCommands.li2: Click the pencil on any chip to edit it, or the × to remove it.
quickCommands.li3: Drag a chip and drop it on another to reorder — the order is the priority, and is saved back to the profile immediately.

filters.heading: Filters
filters.p1: Click Filters in the monitor toolbar. Each rule is a regex, either include (only show matching lines) or exclude (hide matching lines). Rules stack, matches are highlighted, and each rule shows a live count.
filters.example: Example: add an include rule ERROR|WARN to only see errors/warnings, plus an exclude rule heartbeat to additionally hide a noisy keep-alive line.

triggers.heading: Triggers
triggers.p1: Click Triggers to react automatically when a line matches a pattern — no code needed. Each rule is a regex plus one action:
triggers.li1: Send — pattern READY → send AT+INFO\r\n to auto-query the device right after it boots.
triggers.li2: Play sound — pattern ERROR → get an audible alert without watching the screen.
triggers.li3: Write to file — pattern FAIL → append every failing line to its own log file.
triggers.li4: Bookmark line — pattern ERROR → mark every error line so you can jump between them later with the bookmark navigator.

script.heading: Script Engine (Lua)
script.intro: Click Script to write a small Lua program for that tab. Available functions:
script.li1: on_data(line) — define this function and it's called once per received line.
script.li3: wait_for(pattern, timeout_ms) — blocks until a matching line arrives or the timeout elapses; returns the matched line or nil.
script.li5: plot(channel, value) — push a point straight into the plotter, if this tab is its selected source.
script.li6: timer(interval_ms, fn) — run fn repeatedly on a schedule.
script.example1Label: Example — extract a value and auto-reply once ready:
script.sandboxNote: This is a convenience sandbox (no os/io/require, each callback capped at ~2s) for scripts you write yourself — not a hardened boundary against malicious code.

plotter.heading: Plotter
plotter.p1: Toggle the chart icon in the top bar (Ctrl+Shift+P) and pick a source tab. Lines are auto-parsed as CSV (1.2,3.4), space-separated, or key:value pairs (e.g. temp:24.5,hum:51.2) — up to 8 channels. Each channel keeps a sliding window of the most recent points, dropping the oldest once full; Settings → Plotter buffer size controls how many (1,000–500,000, default 50,000) — raise it if you're watching a long-running session and don't want early data pushed out.
plotter.p2: Example — extract a value that isn't in a recognized format: a log line like Current temperature reading: 24.5C won't auto-parse. Open Extractors and add pattern temp=(\d+\.\d+) → channel temp (adjust the pattern to match your actual log text) to pull the number out anyway. A script's plot(...) calls land on the same chart too.
plotter.toolsIntro: The toolbar also has a set of analysis tools:
plotter.math: Math — derived channels computed from real ones: A+B, A−B, A×B, A÷B, moving average, derivative (units/second), or RMS over a sample window. They show up in the legend prefixed with ƒ and behave like normal channels everywhere — stats, FFT, CSV export.
plotter.levels: Levels — horizontal alert lines. Pick a channel and a value; a dashed line is drawn at that level and a beep sounds whenever the channel crosses it upward. Example: channel temp > 80 beeps the moment a temperature reading exceeds 80.
plotter.stats: Stats — a live strip showing each visible channel's min / max / avg / peak-to-peak and an estimated frequency (blank for flat or non-periodic signals).
plotter.fft: FFT — switches the chart to the frequency domain: the current buffer is resampled to a uniform rate, a window function (Hann by default, Hamming or rectangular selectable) is applied, and the amplitude spectrum is shown with Hz on the x axis. Needs at least 64 samples. Note: values between real samples are held constant by the plotter's ingest (a zero-order hold), which adds small artificial harmonics — fine for spotting dominant frequencies, not for precision measurement.
plotter.csvPng: CSV / PNG — export the whole buffer (timestamps + every channel including math channels) as CSV, or the chart as a PNG image (the HTML legend below the chart isn't part of the image).

flashEsp32.heading: Flash ESP32
flashEsp32.step1: Open the flash panel (zap icon or Ctrl+Shift+F), keep ESP32 selected.
flashEsp32.step2: Pick the port and baud rate, then Detect chip.
flashEsp32.step3: Add segments manually — Example: offset 0x1000 for the bootloader, 0x8000 for the partition table, 0x10000 for the app binary (offsets vary by project — check your build output) — or use Smart add (below) to fill these in automatically.
flashEsp32.step4: Click Flash; Erase chip wipes everything first if needed.
flashEsp32.smartAdd: Smart add lets you pick your build output files (bootloader, partitions, firmware, a filesystem image) all at once, and fills in the right offset for each — reading the actual compiled partition table when you include one (partitions.bin/partition-table.bin) rather than guessing, since a filesystem image's offset shifts with flash size and partition scheme and a wrong guess could overwrite the app. The bootloader offset is filled in from the detected chip (0x1000 on original ESP32, 0x0 on S2/S3/C2/C3/C6/H2 — run Detect chip first for this to be exact). If a selected partition table needs an OTA "boot selector" (otadata) image and you didn't include your own boot_app0.bin, EDT supplies its own bundled copy automatically. Anything Smart add can't confidently match is added with a blank offset rather than a guess — fill those in by hand.
flashEsp32.batch: Batch (next to Single) flashes the same segments to several boards at once — check every port to include, then Flash N device(s). Each port flashes on its own, in parallel, with its own live progress/status, so one board failing (or being unplugged) doesn't stop or block the others.
flashEsp32.autoFlash: Auto-flash on plug (in Batch mode) skips the checkbox step entirely: once armed, any newly plugged device that looks like an ESP32 (by its USB serial chip — CP210x, CH340, FTDI, or Espressif's native USB) is flashed immediately with the current baud rate and segments, no per-device confirmation. Meant for repetitive production flashing — plug the next board, it flashes itself. It skips a port that's already open in another tab, and the VID/PID check is only a pre-filter: flashing a non-ESP32 device fails harmlessly at the chip handshake rather than writing anything. Leave it off unless you're actively flashing a batch — it stays armed for as long as the Flash panel is open.
flashEsp32.provision: Provision (third mode, next to Batch) is separate from flashing — it sends a scripted sequence of plain serial commands to an already-flashed device instead of writing firmware. Build a list of steps (each one a command, a line ending, and either "wait for response" with a match string/timeout or a fixed delay), pick a baud rate, then either Run now on a currently-plugged port or arm Auto-run on plug so the workflow fires by itself the moment a matching device is connected — handy for commissioning many boards with the same WiFi credentials/device ID/config commands in a row. Steps and baud rate are remembered across restarts; arming is not, for the same reason auto-flash isn't remembered either.

flashStm32.heading: Flash STM32
flashStm32.intro: Requires STM32CubeProgrammer installed — the panel detects it automatically.
flashStm32.stLink: ST-Link (SWD) — for boards with an on-board or external ST-Link.
flashStm32.uart: UART bootloader — for boards without one; put the MCU in bootloader mode via BOOT0 first.
flashStm32.optionBytes: Option-byte writes (e.g. RDP level) require an explicit confirmation dialog — read it before confirming, as some settings can permanently affect debug access.

app.confirmCloseAllTabs: Opening a project closes every tab currently open. Continue?
app.partialRestoreWarning: {{failed}} of {{total}} connection(s) could not be reopened.

connect.title: New connection
connect.rs485Title: Toggles RTS around each write for RS485 transceivers whose DE/RE pin has no auto-direction circuitry

monitor.clearConfirm: Clear the log buffer for this tab?

flash.title: Flash
flash.smartAddTitle: Pick build output files and auto-fill offsets — reads the real partition table when you include one, instead of guessing
flash.eraseConfirm: Erase the entire chip? This cannot be undone.
flash.smartAddDialogTitle: Select ESP32 build files (bootloader, partitions, firmware, filesystem image…)
flash.partitionParseError: Could not parse partition table, offsets left blank where unsure: {{error}}
flash.unmatchedWarning: {{count}} file(s) couldn't be matched to a known offset — fill those in manually. This usually means the file didn't have a recognizable name, or its role was ambiguous (e.g. multiple app/OTA files without one you selected being an exact match).

mqtt.selectTopicPrompt: Select a topic to see its messages.
mqtt.clearRetainedTitle: Publish an empty retained message to clear it

plot.fftTitle: Frequency-domain view (FFT of the current buffer)
plot.mathTitle: Derived channels computed from existing ones
plot.levelsTitle: Horizontal alert levels — beep when a channel crosses one upward
plot.csvTitle: Export the whole buffer (including math channels) as CSV
plot.pngTitle: Export the chart as a PNG image (legend not included)
plot.extractorPatternPlaceholder: regex with one capture group, e.g. temp=(\d+\.\d+)

macro.noStepsYet: No steps recorded yet — click Record, then send commands normally.

modbus.disabledWhileSlaveListening: Disabled while the Modbus Slave emulator is listening on this tab
modbus.labelTitle: Label — also used as the plotter channel name
modbus.disabledWhilePollingActive: Disabled while Modbus poll rules are enabled on this tab

provision.autoRunLabel: Auto-run on plug (ESP32-like devices run this workflow immediately)
provision.noDevicesYet: No devices provisioned yet this session.

flashBatch.autoFlashLabel: Auto-flash on plug (ESP32-like devices flash immediately, no confirmation)
flashBatch.autoFlashNoSegments: Armed, but no firmware segments configured yet — nothing will flash until you add at least one.

stm32.massEraseConfirm: Mass erase the entire chip? This cannot be undone.
stm32.rdpWarning: WARNING: changing RDP (readout protection) can PERMANENTLY lock debug access to this chip if set incorrectly. This cannot always be undone.
stm32.cliNotFoundDetail: It cannot be bundled with EDT — ST's license does not permit redistributing it. Install STM32CubeProgrammer, then recheck below.

signalBar.dtrTitle: Data Terminal Ready (writable — click to toggle)
signalBar.rtsAutoTitle: Controlled automatically by RS485 half-duplex mode
signalBar.rtsManualTitle: Request To Send (writable — click to toggle)

send.checksumTitle: Appends a checksum to every sent message (suppresses the line ending above when active)
send.textPlaceholder: Type a command and press Enter
